data.title: European Wax Center reports Q4 results
data.description:
• European Wax Center press release (EWCZ): Q4 net loss of $0.59M.
  • Revenue of $45.1M (-9.3% Y/Y) misses by $1.13M.
  • Franchisees opened 1 and closed 7 centers. We ended the quarter with 1,047 centers, representing a 1.9% decrease versus 1,067 centers in the prior year period.
  • System-wide sales of $225.6 million decreased 1.6% from $229.3 million in the prior year period, primarily driven by a shift in service mix.
  • Same-store sales decreased 0.1%.
  •

data.title: Kevin Warsh’s rate-cut plan hits an oil slick
data.description:
Kevin Warsh's new job is looking harder by the day.

The Fed chief-in-waiting is set to take the helm at the central bank at the end of May, assuming the nomination process goes as expected. His first FOMC meeting would come June 16–17.

The White House mission has been to get the fed funds rate lower, and Warsh is expected to move in that direction once he replaces Jay Powell.

But the bond market is currently signaling that inflation worries are building following the attacks on Iran and the surge in oil prices. Rather than acting as a safe haven, as they normally do in times of geopolitical conflict, Treasuries have declined, pushing yields higher.

The two-year yield (US2Y) has risen about 15 basis points since the start of the Middle East conflict to around 3.53%. The benchmark 10-year yield (US10Y), which had been on a steady downward path since early February, has climbed by a similar amount and is back near 4.1%.

Oil reaction is 'massive'

The obvious concern is oil (USO) (BNO), which has spiked more than 10% and could have further to run.

Robert Brooks, senior fellow at the Brookings Institution, says there’s “a weird tendency in markets to downplay unexpected shocks when they happen.”

People don’t like to look like they didn’t see it coming, so they downplay the shock and its potential impact, he said.

What’s happening now in oil “is absolutely massive.”

President Donald Trump has said Operation Epic Fury could last four to five weeks. That leaves a couple of months for things to normalize before Warsh’s first meeting, though Polymarket odds currently predict a cessation of hostilities by the end of June.

SA analyst Jason Vaillancourt of Columbia Threadneedle Investments said that while Iran may lack the capacity to enforce a prolonged full closure of the Strait of Hormuz, even partial disruptions — through shipping delays, higher insurance costs or attacks on regional energy infrastructure — could keep oil prices elevated.

Odds shifting to a midsummer cut

Following the attacks, fed funds futures show a 60% chance that rates stay unchanged after Warsh’s inaugural meeting, up from 54% a week ago and 42% a month ago.

Two quarter-point cuts are still priced in by year-end, though the odds of that outcome have dipped to around 60%.

Minneapolis Fed President Neel Kashkari said it is “too soon to know” what impact a war with Iran might have on inflation or how long any effects could last.

“Coming to this moment, I was feeling pretty good,” Kashkari told Bloomberg. “I felt like policy was in a pretty good place.”

data.title: adidas AG GAAP EPS of €7.46, revenue of €24.81B
data.description:
• adidas AG press release (ADDYY): FY GAAP EPS of €7.46.
  • Revenue of €24.81B (+4.8% Y/Y).
  • The adidas AG Executive and Supervisory Boards will recommend paying a dividend of € 2.80 per dividend-entitled share to shareholders at the Annual General Meeting on May 7, 2026. This represents an increase of 40% compared to the prior year (2025: € 2.00).

data.title: Stevanato Group Non-GAAP EPS of €0.18, revenue of €346.5M
data.description:
• Stevanato Group press release (STVN): Q4 Non-GAAP EPS of €0.18.
  • Revenue of €346.5M (+4.8% Y/Y).
  • The Company is establishing its fiscal 2026 guidance. The Company expects revenue in the range of €1.26 billion to €1.29 billion, adjusted EBITDA in the range of €331.8 million to €346.9 million, and adjusted diluted EPS in the range of €0.59 to €0.63.

data.title: Babcock & Wilcox Enterprises GAAP EPS of -$0.05 beats by $0.02, revenue of $161M beats by $5.4M
data.description:
• Babcock & Wilcox Enterprises press release (BW): Q4 GAAP EPS of -$0.05 beats by $0.02.
  • Revenue of $161M (-0.5% Y/Y) beats by $5.4M.
  • Adjusted EBITDA from Continuing Operations in the fourth quarter of $16.4 million, a 53% increase compared to the same period of 2024.
  • Parts & services revenues increased 17% in 2025, continuing to outperform expectations due to increased coal generation usage and higher baseload demand in North America.
  • Total global pipeline continues to grow and now exceeds $12.0 billion
  • Continuing Operations Backlog of $2.8 billion, including the $2.4 billion data center project
  • Significantly reduced debt on balance sheet, resulting in net debt of $119.7 million

data.title: Rise Gold partners for Idaho-Maryland mine project
data.description:
• Rise Gold (RYES) has entered into an 18-month strategic development agreement with Morgan Hughes Energy to advance its Idaho-Maryland Mine project as a U.S.-based gold and critical-minerals project. 

  • The company will issue 18M warrants to Morgan Hughes at a $0.40 strike price, vesting in stages tied to milestones such as critical-minerals program advancement and capital commitments.

  • A $1.5M milestone payment applies if major funding is secured within 36 months; otherwise, a $250,000 reimbursement or equivalent warrants follows.

  • "The I-M Mine is an environmentally sound, historic mine that would create 475 new local jobs, become Nevada County's largest taxpayer, and supply the U.S. military with a critical mineral necessary for national defense, " said CEO David Watkinson.

data.title: Levi Strauss officially ends the Dockers era at the company
data.description:
Levi Strauss & Co. (LEVI)  announced the final closing of its previously disclosed sale of the Dockers brand to Authentic Brands Group.

The company said the divestiture will sharpen its focus on the Levi’s brand and Beyond Yoga, simplifying the portfolio and strengthening the structural profitability. The transaction also aligns the company’s assets with its highest-return growth opportunities and reinforces its disciplined capital allocation approach. Due to an already strong cash position, Levi Strauss (LEVI) highlighted that it can return the net cash proceeds to shareholders through existing accelerated share repurchase programs while maintaining flexibility to invest in long-term growth.

"The Dockers transaction further aligns our portfolio with our strategic priorities, focusing on our direct-to-consumer first approach, growing our international presence, and investing in opportunities across women’s and denim lifestyle," stated Levi Strauss (LEVI) CEO Michelle Gass at the time the deal was announced. "After a robust process, we are confident that we maximized the value of the business and that Authentic is the right organization to usher in the next chapter of growth for the Dockers brand," she added.

The Dockers brand was launched by Levi Strauss & Co. (LEVI) in 1986 and quickly became synonymous with business casual attire, especially khaki pants. The brand capitalized on the growing popularity of “Casual Fridays” in American offices during the 1990s, transforming workplace fashion by promoting khakis as acceptable professional wear. The brand expanded internationally in the early 1990s and introduced a women's line. Dockers' annual revenue for 2024 was $323.2M.

data.title: Kostohryz favors Brent crude over US oil stocks amid Middle East war risks
data.description:
Seeking Alpha analyst James Kostohryz is positioning his portfolios heavily in Brent crude oil rather than U.S. oil equities as tensions escalate between the United States, Israel, and Iran.

Speaking on the Seeking Alpha podcast, Kostohryz explained that while energy stocks remain fundamentally undervalued, he prefers direct exposure to the commodity through the Brent benchmark ETF (BNO) over the U.S.-focused West Texas Intermediate benchmark (USO)

“We’re more exposed to the commodity right now than we are to the producers,” Kostohryz said, noting that his service recently shifted from the U.S. Oil Fund (USO) to the Brent-focused alternative.

The strategist cited concerns that the Trump administration could reintroduce oil export bans or price controls that would depress U.S. oil prices relative to global benchmarks. Under such a scenario, even with Brent crude prices exceeding $200, American benchmark prices could remain below $100.

For oil equities, Kostohryz recommends investors look beyond North American producers to companies operating in Latin America, Africa, Asia, and Australia. He warns that US and Canadian producers face additional regulatory risk if export restrictions return.

The strategist cautioned that while oil stocks may appear cheap on forward earnings at elevated oil prices, those valuations could prove misleading. “If oil prices come back to $70 within a year or two or three or four, the PE that looks like a seven PE when oil is at $100… might be 25 looking three years out,” he explained.

Kostohryz believes the Iran conflict could push Brent crude above $100 if disruptions last more than a month and potentially above $200 if the crisis extends beyond two months—scenarios he considers significantly underpriced by current markets.

ETFs: (USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (XLE)

data.title: Markets AM Need to Know: Trump on crypto bill, Canada on Middle East tensions, and more
data.description:
Stock index futures were little changed on Wednesday as traders continued to monitor developments in the Middle East and awaited the ADP Employment Report.

Now, here are five news stories that broke overnight to watch out for:

OpenAI considers NATO deployment: Microsoft (MSFT)-backed OpenAI is in discussions over a contract to deploy its AI technology across the North Atlantic Treaty Organization’s unclassified networks, according to The Wall Street Journal. The report noted that OpenAI CEO Sam Altman had initially told staff the ChatGPT maker was looking to deploy on all NATO classified networks, though the current scope appears limited to unclassified systems. OpenAI did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

U.S. Navy warns of Chinese undersea threat: Senior U.S. naval officials testified before the U.S.-China Economic and Security Review Commission that China’s next wave of submarines could allow Beijing to threaten more of the U.S. mainland while operating closer to its shores. Navy leaders said China is rapidly modernizing its submarine fleet, moving toward more advanced nuclear-powered boats armed with longer-range ballistic missiles, underscoring what Washington sees as an intensifying undersea rivalry between the two powers.

Senate to test Trump’s Iran strategy: The U.S. Senate is set to vote Wednesday on President Donald Trump’s decision to launch military action against Iran, marking a major congressional test as the conflict rapidly expands across the Middle East without a clearly defined U.S. exit strategy. The legislation, known as a war powers resolution, gives lawmakers an opportunity to demand congressional approval before any further attacks are carried out.

Trump meets Coinbase CEO to push crypto bill: President Donald Trump met privately on Tuesday with Coinbase (COIN) CEO Brian Armstrong before publicly backing the company’s stance in an ongoing lobbying dispute with banks that has stalled a major cryptocurrency bill, Politico reported. Trump later wrote on Truth Social that banks “need to make a good deal with the crypto industry" in order to advance digital asset legislation that has stalled on Capitol Hill.

Canada urges Middle East de-escalation: Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney said his support for U.S. military action in Iran came “with regret,” calling the conflict another sign of the failure of the international order. Speaking at a news conference in Sydney, Carney said Canada calls for a rapid de-escalation of hostilities and urged all parties, including the United States and Israel, to respect the rules of international engagement. Canada said it is prepared to assist in achieving de-escalation.

data.title: Gold looks overbought despite geopolitical tensions, Kostohryz says
data.description:
Despite escalating tensions between the US, Israel, and Iran, gold may not be the safe haven many investors expect, according to Seeking Alpha James Kostohryz.

In a recent interview, Kostohryz argued that gold has already priced in significant crisis risk and appears vulnerable to a decline, even as geopolitical uncertainty intensifies in the Middle East.

“Gold right now is higher than it has been at any time in the last 400 years” in real purchasing power terms, Kostohryz noted. “This gives you an indication of the fact that gold is pricing in some sort of major long-term inflationary scenario.”

The strategist pointed to troubling technical signals as evidence of gold’s vulnerability. On a recent trading day when crude oil prices surged approximately 8%, gold declined nearly 4%—the opposite of what investors would typically expect during a risk-off environment.

“When you have gold falling on a day where it should be rising, that’s really bad news for gold investors,” Kostohryz explained.

While acknowledging that gold could potentially rally to $6,000 if the crisis intensifies, Kostohryz said he has exited his gold positions because the metal is “pricing in too much doom and gloom.” He expressed skepticism about extreme scenarios being factored into current prices, stating he doesn’t believe the current crisis signals “the end of the dollar” or the collapse of fiat currency regimes worldwide.

For investors seeking inflation protection without gold’s current risks, Kostohryz suggested Treasury Inflation Protected Securities ((TIPS)) as an alternative that provides direct exposure to long-term inflation without the speculative premium embedded in gold prices.

For more Seeking Alpha podcasts, click here.
